one_line_takeaway: Tex3D shows that physically realizable, gradient-optimized 3D object textures are a far more robust and effective adversarial attack surface against VLA robot-control models than 2D image patches, pushing task failure rates up to 96.7% and surviving both viewpoint changes and standard input-space defenses.
key_finding: On OpenVLA, Tex3D raises the average LIBERO task failure rate from 24.1% (clean) to 88.1% (untargeted) and 90.5% (targeted); on OpenVLA-OFT from 4.7% to 76.0%/79.3%; on pi0 from 4.6% to 71.8%/73.3%; on pi0.5 from 2.8% to 69.3%/71.2%. The single highest reported number is 96.7% on the OpenVLA Spatial task under the targeted setting (from a 15.6% clean baseline). Ablations show each component matters: on OpenVLA Spatial (untargeted), failure rate climbs from 24.6% (Gaussian) to 75.1% (Single-frame) to 80.5% (Vertex Param.) to 90.3% (Tex+Temp.) to 95.8% (full Tex3D). Targeted-attack precision is confirmed by low average L1 distance to the target action: 0.0176 (OpenVLA), 0.0237 (OpenVLA-OFT), 0.0305 (pi0), 0.0372 (pi0.5). Cross-model transfer is strong: OpenVLA to OpenVLA-OFT transfers reach 61.5-70.6% failure rate; pi0 to pi0.5 reach 49.2-58.4%; cross-family transfer (OpenVLA to pi0/pi0.5) still reaches 27.8-44.1%. Against 2D patch baselines, Tex3D is markedly more robust to viewpoint/geometry changes (staying 80.8-88.1% across camera-angle/rotation/position variations vs. 2D patch dropping from 100% to 63.4-67.4%) and to physical deployment (66.8-67.6% vs. 40.8-56.6% for 2D patch/2D patch+EoT). Tex3D is largely unaffected by four common input-space defenses (JPEG, noise, median blur, bit-depth reduction), staying at 86.6-87.3% failure rate. The dual-renderer (FBD) pipeline introduces at most a 1.2-point discrepancy versus MuJoCo-only rendering, and SSIM between the two renderers' outputs is consistently ~0.999+, supporting the claim that attack success stems from the adversarial texture rather than rendering artifacts.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Tex3D, the first framework for end-to-end optimization of physically realizable adversarial 3D textures directly within a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) simulation loop. It contributes two novel techniques: (1) Foreground-Background Decoupling (FBD), a dual-renderer pipeline that keeps MuJoCo running unmodified for the background/physics while re-rendering the target object in the differentiable renderer Nvdiffrast, with cross-renderer alignment of geometric (MVP transform) and lighting parameters (I_a, I_d, rho) so gradients can flow from the VLA action loss back to the object's texture map; and (2) Trajectory-Aware Adversarial Optimization (TAAO), which (a) scores frame 'criticality' via latent velocity/acceleration of a pretrained visual encoder's features (central differences, jointly normalized, combined via max, then turned into per-frame weights with a temperature-scaled softmax) to concentrate optimization on behaviorally pivotal moments (e.g., grasp onset), and (b) reparameterizes the adversarial texture as per-vertex color attributes (recovered via barycentric interpolation) rather than optimizing the full pixel-space texture map, constraining the perturbation to a smooth, low-rank manifold to reduce overfitting and improve transferability. On top of FBD+TAAO the paper instantiates untargeted attacks (maximize action deviation from the clean reference action) and targeted attacks (minimize deviation from an attacker-chosen target trajectory), and adds an Expectation-over-Transformations (EoT) scheme (3D pose/viewpoint/distance perturbations plus 2D brightness/contrast/blur augmentations) to improve sim-to-real transfer for physical deployment.
threat_model: White-box, gradient-based attacker with differentiable access to the victim VLA policy's action outputs (attack requires backpropagating the action loss through the model to the texture), operating within an embodied manipulation simulator (MuJoCo/LIBERO) and validated on a real Franka Emika Panda robot. The attack surface is the physical appearance (3D surface texture) of a manipulable object in the scene, not the language instruction and not a 2D image patch overlaid post-hoc. The attacker crafts an adversarial texture offline (via the FBD+TAAO optimization loop) and then applies it to the real object (via 3D printing in the physical experiments) or to the simulated mesh; deployment requires no further access to the model at inference time, only to the object's texture. Both untargeted (cause any task failure) and targeted (redirect the gripper to a specific alternate target) attack goals are considered. Cross-model transferability (attacking one VLA and evaluating against another) is also tested, implying a partial black-box/transfer threat setting for the deployment phase even though optimization itself is white-box.
limitations: The paper's own text (through the sections extracted) does not include a dedicated 'Limitations' section within the main body (may appear in an appendix beyond the extracted 22 pages of a 7-page PDF, which was fully covered here). Notable caveats visible in the text itself: (1) the optimization is white-box/gradient-based, requiring differentiable access to the victim model at attack-crafting time, which is a stronger assumption than pure black-box attacks; (2) evaluation is restricted to LIBERO-style tabletop pick-and-place manipulation tasks and four specific VLA architectures (OpenVLA, OpenVLA-OFT, pi0, pi0.5); generalization to other embodied tasks (e.g., locomotion, dexterous manipulation, outdoor robotics) is not evaluated; (3) real-world validation uses 3D-printed objects and a single Franka Panda arm with one monocular camera and a limited set of pick-and-place tasks (100 trials each), which is a controlled, favorable physical setting rather than diverse in-the-wild deployment; (4) the defense evaluation only tests input-space/image-processing defenses (JPEG, noise, blur, bit-depth) and does not test adversarial training, certified defenses, or detection-based defenses against 3D textures specifically.

datasets_used: LIBERO benchmark (Spatial, Object, Goal, Long-horizon task suites)
quantitative_results: OpenVLA average task failure rate: 24.1% (clean) -> 88.1% (Tex3D untargeted) -> 90.5% (Tex3D targeted)
Peak result: OpenVLA Spatial task failure rate 15.6% (clean) -> 95.8% (untargeted) / 96.7% (targeted)
Targeted-attack L1 distance to target action (avg): OpenVLA 0.0176, OpenVLA-OFT 0.0237, pi0 0.0305, pi0.5 0.0372
Cross-model transfer OpenVLA->OpenVLA-OFT: 61.5-70.6% failure rate; OpenVLA-OFT->OpenVLA: 70.2-75.7%
Dual-renderer (MuJoCo+Nvdiffrast) fidelity vs MuJoCo-only: at most 1.2 percentage-point discrepancy across four task suites
vs 2D patch baseline under geometric variation: Tex3D stays 80.8-88.1% while 2D patch drops from 100% to 63.4-67.4%
Physical-world failure rate: Tex3D 66.8-67.6% vs 2D patch 40.8-50.8% vs 2D patch+EoT 49.8-56.6%
Robustness to input-space defenses (JPEG, noise, median blur, bit-depth reduction): Tex3D failure rate stays ~86.6-87.3%
LPIPS perceptual distance: Tex3D 0.012-0.014 vs 2D patch-based 0.031-0.033 (lower = less perceptible)
Ablation on OpenVLA Spatial (untargeted): Gaussian 24.6% -> Single-frame 75.1% -> Vertex Param. 80.5% -> Tex+Temp. 90.3% -> full Tex3D 95.8%
baselines_compared: Tex+Temp. (ablation: temporal consistency without full TAAO)
